_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Wilcox County, AL. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Wilcox County (formed 1819, seat at Camden) has experienced some record losses but retains most records from formation. Home of the Gee's Bend community, whose documentary record is uniquely shaped by geographic isolation.

current_courthouse.location: Camden, AL — Broad Street. The antebellum courthouse remains in use.

previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the 1857 courthouse. Records transferred.

record_losses.cause: Aging and minor damage — no catastrophic fire
record_losses.description: Wilcox County's courthouse has not experienced a major fire. The 1857 building survived the Civil War. Bound record volumes survive from the 1820s. Some early loose papers are missing or damaged by age and humidity.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Wilcox County's records are largely intact. For {ancestor_name} at Gee's Bend, the challenge is not lost records but the community's isolation — Gee's Bend families had minimal interaction with the Camden courthouse, and their documentary footprint in county records is correspondingly thin.
record_losses.source: Alabama Department of Archives and History. Guide to Alabama County Records.

deeds.gaps: Minor gaps in earliest volumes. Gee's Bend families had limited land ownership until the New Deal era, so deed records for the community are sparse before the 1940s.
deeds.repository: Wilcox County Probate Office; ADAH microfilm

wills_estates.completeness: Good — Pettway family estate records are essential for documenting the enslaved community at Gee's Bend

marriages.completeness: Good for white marriages. Freedmen's marriages from 1865 — Gee's Bend community marriages may be underrepresented due to isolation and limited access to Camden.
marriages.gaps: Gee's Bend community marriages may not appear in county records if performed by community ministers without formal licensing.
marriages.repository: Wilcox County Probate Office; ADAH; Freedmen's Bureau at NARA

vital_records.completeness: Very spotty — Wilcox County's rural isolation meant compliance with vital registration was lower than in more urban counties. Gee's Bend community vital events are significantly underrepresented.
vital_records.repository: Alabama Center for Health Statistics; ADAH

substitute_sources.description: The Pettway family's plantation at Gee's Bend generated records — estate inventories, tax records, and land transactions — that document the enslaved community. The Pettway estate records list enslaved individuals who are the ancestors of the current Gee's Bend community.
substitute_sources.repository: Wilcox County Probate Office; ADAH; some private family papers
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracing Gee's Bend families to their enslaved origins. The Pettway estate inventories name individuals by name, age, and sometimes family groupings.
substitute_sources.source: Beardsley et al., The Quilts of Gee's Bend (2002).

substitute_sources.description: Bureau records from the Camden area, including records related to the Gee's Bend community.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for the slavery-to-freedom transition at Gee's Bend and throughout Wilcox County.
substitute_sources.source: NARA, Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands, RG 105.

substitute_sources.source_type: New Deal / Farm Security Administration records
substitute_sources.description: Gee's Bend was the site of a New Deal resettlement project in the 1930s–1940s. The Farm Security Administration documented the community through photographs, surveys, and project records.
substitute_sources.repository: Library of Congress (FSA photographs); NARA (FSA project files); ADAH
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Unique documentation of Gee's Bend families — names, household compositions, economic conditions, and photographs. The FSA records may be the most complete documentation of the community between the Freedmen's Bureau era and the civil rights movement.
substitute_sources.source: Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division, Farm Security Administration Collection.

substitute_sources.description: The scholarly and artistic documentation of the Gee's Bend quilters produced extensive interviews, genealogies, and community histories.
substitute_sources.repository: Tinwood Books / Souls Grown Deep Foundation; published in 'The Quilts of Gee's Bend' (2002) and related volumes
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The quilting documentation includes detailed family genealogies constructed from oral history and supplemented by documentary research. For Gee's Bend families, these published genealogies may be the most accessible source of family history information.
substitute_sources.source: Beardsley et al., The Quilts of Gee's Bend (2002). Arnett et al., Gee's Bend: The Women and Their Quilts (2002).

narrative_hooks: Wilcox County's courthouse at Camden has stood since 1857 — but for {ancestor_name}'s family at Gee's Bend, the courthouse might as well have been on the moon when the ferry was not running.

The Pettway estate inventories at the Wilcox County courthouse list the enslaved people of Gee's Bend by name — {ancestor_name}'s ancestors appear in those documents as property, the only official acknowledgment of their existence before emancipation.

The Farm Security Administration photographed and documented Gee's Bend families in the 1930s–1940s — if {ancestor_name} was at Gee's Bend, the Library of Congress may hold photographs of the family and the cabin.

Wilcox County vital records are spotty even by Alabama standards — Gee's Bend's isolation meant that births and deaths went unregistered for decades. The absence of a certificate is the norm, not the exception.
source_references: Alabama Department of Archives and History. Guide to Alabama County Records.
Beardsley, John, et al. The Quilts of Gee's Bend. Atlanta: Tinwood Books, 2002.
National Archives and Records Administration. Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands, Record Group 105.
Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division. Farm Security Administration Collection.
